title: 👑 Elite challenge ⭐⭐⭐⭐: the health champion's test

questions.prompt: Complete: "For a healthy body, eat ___ vegetables, but don't drink too ___ soda."
questions.explanation: Two choices in one sentence: "vegetables" can be counted, so it takes "many"; "soda" cannot be counted, so it takes "much" → many vegetables … much soda ✓. The common trap is using the same word twice — you must judge each noun on its own: countable → many, uncountable → much.

questions.prompt: Complete: "There is not a single cloud. The sky is blue and it is very ___."
questions.explanation: Noun + y gives the weather adjective, and a short word like "sun" doubles its last letter: sun → sunny ✓. The common trap is "suny" with a single n; "sunly" wrongly uses -ly, and "sun" is the noun, not the adjective.

questions.prompt: One sentence has a mistake. Which one? (1) You mustn't run near the pool. (2) Let's play tennis after school. (3) What about play basketball? (4) Don't eat too many sweets.
questions.explanation: This question asks for the faulty sentence. "What about play basketball?" is wrong: after "What about" you need a verb in -ing → What about playing basketball? ✓. Sentences 1, 2 and 4 are correct: mustn't + base verb, Let's + base verb, and "many" with countable sweets.

questions.prompt: A sign at the pool says: "Danger! Deep water." Which rule is correct?

options.text: You mustn't swim here without an adult.

questions.explanation: Deep, dangerous water means swimming alone is forbidden, so the safe rule is "You mustn't swim here without an adult" ✓. "You must swim here alone" gives the dangerous, wrong meaning; "don't must" and "mustn't to" are both impossible forms — mustn't is followed by the base verb, with no "to".

questions.prompt: Complete: "Yesterday, Lina ___ a bad cold, so she ___ at home all day."
questions.explanation: "Yesterday" needs the simple past. "Have" is irregular → had, and "rest" is regular → rested (add -ed): she had a cold, so she rested ✓. The common trap is "haved" — irregular verbs never take -ed — or forgetting the -ed on the regular verb "rest".

questions.prompt: In which word is the letter "l" silent?
questions.explanation: In "half" the l is written but not said — we pronounce /hɑːf/, like the silent l in "walk" and "talk" ✓. The common trap is trusting the spelling: in "clean", "leg" and "healthy" the l is clearly pronounced.
